title: Accelerating End-to-End Co-Folding Performance with NVIDIA BioNeMo Agent Toolkit | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA's BioNeMo Agent Toolkit chains GPU-accelerated MSA search (177x faster than CPU JackHMMER), cuEquivariance-accelerated OpenFold3 inference (up to 3x faster, longer sequences), and a new context-parallel technique called Fold-CP (32,000-token complexes across 64 B300 GPUs) into an agent-composable pipeline, arguing these are qualitative unlocks — not just speedups — that make previously intractable structural biology problems like ribosome-scale complex prediction and early-stage virtual screening computationally tractable.

insights.claim: Co-folding structure prediction is now a mainstream, large-scale drug-discovery workload increasingly driven end-to-end by AI agents, so every pipeline step (MSA generation, inference, serving, multi-GPU scale-out) must be fast and scalable since a bottleneck anywhere limits overall throughput.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: Frames NVIDIA's value proposition in computational biology as full-pipeline acceleration rather than a single point-solution, which is a stickier, harder-to-replace offering for pharma/biotech customers building agentic discovery workflows.
insights.quote: For an agent to run that pipeline well, every step needs to be fast and scalable: Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) generation, co-folding inference, serving, and multi-GPU scale-out. A bottleneck anywhere limits overall throughput.

insights.claim: Co-folding model runtime scales cubically with the number of amino acid residues, and single-GPU memory places a hard ceiling on the size of molecular complexes that can be predicted in one shot, making both speed and memory efficiency critical constraints for predicting large molecular assemblies.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: The cubic scaling law explains why memory and compute acceleration compound rather than add for large complexes — a key technical reason large pharma/biotech customers need NVIDIA-class infrastructure rather than commodity compute for large-assembly modeling.
insights.quote: Speed is also important for predicting large molecular assemblies involving multiple proteins and thousands of amino acid residues, as co-folding model runtime scales cubically with the number of residues.

insights.claim: Co-folding models often produce the best predicted structures but can be too computationally expensive to run at the scale of virtual screening (millions to billions of compounds against one or a few targets), which is precisely the gap NVIDIA's acceleration stack is positioned to close.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific commercial use case (large-scale virtual screening) where cost/speed has historically excluded the most accurate models — acceleration here directly expands the addressable market for co-folding-based drug discovery.
insights.quote: While co-folding models often give the best predicted structures, they can be expensive to run, making them impractical for virtual screening applications.

insights.claim: MMseqs2-GPU's Nature Methods paper reports up to 177x faster alignment than CPU JackHMMER on a single L40S GPU, and NVIDIA's benchmarking shows the MSA stage scaling near-linearly past 10,000 tokens on both H100 and B300 (429s vs 463s at ~10k tokens).
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A headline benchmark number (177x) for a step that has traditionally been a CPU-bound bottleneck dominating wall-clock time in structure prediction pipelines — a strong quantified case for GPU infrastructure spend in computational biology.
insights.quote: The MSA Search NIM uses MMseqs2-GPU, whose Nature Methods paper reports up to 177× faster alignment than CPU JackHMMER on a single L40S.
insights.locator: Remove the MSA bottleneck with GPU MSA

insights.claim: GPU acceleration improvements to MMseqs2 (Hopper/Blackwell-specific optimizations, Grace-system support for larger-than-GPU-memory database search, and CUDA 13.2 Blackwell DPX instruction speedups) have been upstreamed back into the main open-source MMseqs2 repository, benefiting the whole community rather than being kept proprietary.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: NVIDIA's strategy of upstreaming GPU optimizations into widely-used open-source bioinformatics tools (rather than gating them behind proprietary NIMs) broadens organic adoption of NVIDIA-optimized code paths across the field, indirectly cementing GPU dependency in academic and industry workflows.
insights.quote: These GPU contributions have been upstreamed back into the main MMseqs2 repository so the whole community can benefit from the accelerations.
insights.locator: Remove the MSA bottleneck with GPU MSA

insights.claim: NVIDIA explicitly warns that the publicly hosted MSA Search NIM endpoint on build.nvidia.com is not built to handle large-volume requests and will time out, recommending the self-hosted NIM as the preferred endpoint for large-scale workloads.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A practical operational caveat: production-scale biotech/pharma customers running large virtual screening campaigns need self-hosted infrastructure (NVIDIA GPUs on-prem or in their own cloud), not the free hosted tier, which is a direct driver of NVIDIA compute/software sales.
insights.quote: build.nvidia.com endpoints are not built to handle large volume requests and will time-out.
insights.locator: Remove the MSA bottleneck with GPU MSA

themes.theme: cuEquivariance and OpenFold3 NIM inference speedups

insights.claim: cuEquivariance, a CUDA-X library of geometric learning primitives providing accelerated Triangle Attention, Triangle Multiplication and Attention Pair Bias kernels, cuts OpenFold3 forward-pass latency by up to ~3x on B300, with the speedup growing from 1.8x at 1,024 tokens to 3.1x at 2,048 tokens as sequence length increases.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: Speedup scaling with sequence length (1.8x to 3.1x) shows cuEquivariance's advantage compounds precisely where it matters most — larger, more biologically realistic complexes — making it disproportionately valuable for frontier drug-discovery use cases.

insights.claim: cuEquivariance kernels extend the maximum sequence length OpenFold3 can handle to ~5.9k tokens, while unaccelerated PyTorch runs out of memory beyond roughly 1.5k-2.5k tokens.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A memory-capacity unlock (not just speed) — roughly 2.5-4x longer sequences become feasible at all, directly expanding which biological complexes are computationally tractable, a capability gap versus unaccelerated open-source baselines.
insights.locator: Fold at SOTA speed with cuEquivariance and the OpenFold3 NIM

insights.claim: Because cuEquivariance accelerations are upstreamed as an optional dependency directly into open-source co-folding models (OpenFold3, OpenFold2, RosettaFold3, Protenix, Boltz), researchers get the speedups automatically simply by running their existing model on an NVIDIA GPU, without switching frameworks.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A frictionless-adoption mechanism: NVIDIA captures performance-driven GPU demand across the entire open-source structural biology model ecosystem, not just its own OpenFold3 NIM, widening its addressable footprint in computational biology.
insights.quote: Because the accelerations are upstreamed into these OSS models, a researcher gets the speedups automatically simply by running the model they already use on an NVIDIA GPU.
insights.locator: Fold at SOTA speed with cuEquivariance and the OpenFold3 NIM

insights.claim: Layering additional inference optimizations on top of cuEquivariance, the OpenFold3 NIM achieves sequence lengths of up to ~6,400 tokens on a single B300 GPU, delivering over 3x speedup on H100 and over 4x on B300 versus baseline.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: The compounding of NIM-level optimizations on top of cuEquivariance (3x -> 4x+) demonstrates NVIDIA's layered software stack strategy: each product tier (library, then packaged microservice) adds further captured value on the same hardware generation.

themes.theme: Fold-CP: scaling beyond a single GPU

insights.claim: Fold-CP introduces context-parallel inference where per-GPU memory requirement scales as O(N²/P) (N=token count, P=GPU count), reaching 32,000-token complexes on 64 B300 GPUs with the Boltz-2 model — about a 12x jump over the single-GPU limit.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A quantified 12x capacity jump via multi-GPU parallelism is a concrete example of NVIDIA turning a hard single-chip memory ceiling into a scale-out software problem, directly monetizable via multi-GPU cluster sales for the largest structural biology problems.
insights.quote: Fold-CP introduces a new parallelization technique such that per-device memory requirement scales as O(N²/P)... reaching 32,000 tokens on 64 B300 with the Boltz-2 model—about a 12× jump over the single-GPU limit.
insights.locator: Scale beyond one GPU with Fold-CP

insights.claim: Folding a complex of roughly 10,000 residues (approximately the scale of the bacterial ribosome) would have been prohibitively expensive or simply out of reach on a single GPU, but becomes tractable across a multi-GPU node when combining B300's larger HBM with Fold-CP.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: A concrete, named biological benchmark (ribosome-scale, ~10,000 residues) gives investors and scientists an intuitive yardstick for what NVIDIA's acceleration stack newly unlocks, beyond abstract token counts.
insights.quote: To make this concrete, folding a complex of ~10,000 residues, roughly the scale of the bacterial ribosome, would have been prohibitively expensive or simply out of reach on a single GPU; on B300 with Fold-CP, such predictions become tractable across a multi-GPU node.

insights.claim: The author explicitly frames these accelerations as qualitative shifts in what questions structural biology can computationally ask, not merely throughput improvements — enabling structure-based methods to be applied earlier in drug discovery campaigns and at much larger compound-library scales than before.
insights.speaker: Kyle Tretina
insights.so_what: This is the article's central investment thesis: NVIDIA's compute/software stack isn't just making existing drug discovery cheaper, it's expanding which drug discovery strategies are viable at all (earlier structure-based screening, larger assemblies), a market-expansion argument rather than a cost-reduction one.
insights.quote: These are qualitative shifts in the questions that structural biology can ask computationally, not merely improvements in throughput.
